title: Selling Digital Art Without PayPal: An Honest Side-by-Side Breakdown — No PayPal Required
author: Unchained Commerce
excerpt: PayPal and Stripe block creators in dozens of countries. Here is the infrastructure that works everywhere they don't.

content.text: You're a talented digital artist, but PayPal's restrictions are suffocating your sales. Every digital art sale should be a smooth experience, not a frustrating runaround of restricted countries and payment gatekeepers. You're not alone – Nigeria's digital creators face this same PayPal blockade. It's time to cut through the corporate spin and get you paid. Let's compare the actual options for selling digital art without PayPal: Gumroad, Payhip, conventional payment processors, and a crypto-native alternative. Which one will get you paid, hassle-free?
What Each Option Actually Offers

Gumroad and Payhip offer basic infrastructure for selling digital products, but they're still stuck in the traditional payment processor world. You'll need a Stripe or PayPal account to receive payments, which is exactly what gets restricted in countries like Nigeria. Conventional payment processors like Stripe and PayPal provide a wider range of features, but they're also the ones imposing those pesky restrictions. A crypto-native alternative like Unchained Commerce's Full Stack Bundle offers a backend with automated delivery, payment verification, and PDF receipts, all without requiring a payment processor account. This setup allows for instant automated delivery, no payment processor account required, and a range of features tailored for digital creators.
How They Handle Restricted Countries

Gumroad and Payhip can't process payments for restricted countries like Nigeria, which means your sales will get blocked. Conventional payment processors like Stripe and PayPal also restrict payments due to geographic, banking, or KYC limitations. A crypto-native alternative like Unchained Commerce's Full Stack Bundle is designed to bypass these restrictions, using NOWPayments as a payment processor that supports over 300 coins and doesn't require KYC or a buyer account. This setup allows for hassle-free payments in restricted countries, giving you the freedom to focus on creating and selling your art.
Payment Speed, Fees, and Real Costs

Traditional payment processors like Stripe and PayPal charge varying fees, usually ranging from 2.9% + 30 cents per transaction. Gumroad and Payhip also charge fees, typically 3.5% + 30 cents per sale. A crypto-native alternative like Unchained Commerce's Full Stack Bundle charges a one-time payment of $79.99 for the Full Stack Bundle, with no recurring fees or subscription costs. This setup offers a clear, upfront cost with no hidden expenses, allowing you to budget your expenses accurately and focus on growing your business.
Don't let PayPal's restrictions dictate your creative business.
Automation and Delivery Capabilities

Gumroad and Payhip provide basic infrastructure for selling digital products, but they're limited in automation capabilities. Conventional payment processors like Stripe and PayPal offer more advanced features, but they're still tied to traditional payment processing. A crypto-native alternative like Unchained Commerce's Full Stack Bundle offers instant automated delivery, payment verification, and PDF receipts, all without requiring a payment processor account. This setup gives you the flexibility to automate your sales process, reducing administrative burdens and allowing you to focus on creating and selling your art.
Which One to Choose Based on Your Situation

If you value flexibility and hassle-free payments in restricted countries, a crypto-native alternative like Unchained Commerce's Full Stack Bundle might be the best choice. If you're already invested in traditional payment processors like Stripe and PayPal, sticking with what you know might be the easiest option. However, if you want a clear, upfront cost with no hidden expenses and the ability to automate your sales process, a crypto-native alternative is worth considering.
